result:
I keep coming back to the idea that everything is reheated—ideas, feelings, even the stars. The cosmic microwave background is literally the dim afterglow of the Big Bang, still keeping space a tepid 2.725 Kelvin, so the whole universe is like day-old coffee kept vaguely warm on the burner of its own memory. We’re just bacteria in the foam on top, arguing over whose latte art looks more like the Virgin Mary.

Inside that foam, we keep building miniature universes called “years.” Every January we swear we’ll stop burning ourselves on the same emotional stovetop, and every December we discover new scar tissue shaped like our ex’s initials or our parents’ expectations. The calendar is a branded non-stick pan: nothing sticks, nothing lasts, but the coating itself flakes off in carcinogenic confetti we keep inhaling because it sparkles.

Yesterday I watched a crow unscrew a jar lid on YouTube. The algorithm next served me a video titled “How to Forgive Yourself,” as if those two things were related, as if the corvid’s triumphant caw were the soundtrack to my inner rehabilitation. I clicked anyway. The host said, “Picture your mistake as a red balloon, then let it go.” I pictured the balloon yanked downward by a crow that proceeded to peck it to shreds on a power line, causing a small transformer explosion that blacked out an entire suburb of my subconscious. The lights are still flickering; somewhere a fridge full of hope is quietly thawing.

Meanwhile my phone’s weather app insists it’s “partly cloudy,” which feels like a moral judgment. Partly cloudy: part hope, part obstruction. The sun is that friend who shows up to the group project but never pulls their weight, and we keep giving them credit because at least they showed. I walk outside anyway, half expecting the sky to unzip like a gym bag and dump forgotten socks and high-school report cards all over the sidewalk. Instead I get pollen. Lots of pollen. The trees are writing their memoirs in dust on the windshields of strangers: “We were here before you, we’ll be here after, and we reproduce by vandalism.”

Back indoors, I re-read a childhood book and discover the margins are full of prophecies I wrote in smeared pencil: “By 25 I will know everything.” I’m 34; I can’t even remember where I left my water bottle, which feels like a minor oracle in itself—hydration eluding me the way certainty does. I imagine future-me finding this page, laughing in whatever voice synthetic vocal cords or uploaded consciousness allows. Maybe he’ll annotate: “Still looking, kid. The bottle turned into a river and the river became time. Drink slowly.”

I think the bravest thing we do is keep metabolizing the past. Neurons literally unspool protein memories, break them down, reuse the amino acids to build new thoughts, same way demolished stadiums become paper clips and subway tracks. Every time you remember your first kiss, your brain is cannibalizing an older version of that kiss, like a medieval monk scraping ink off a parchment so he can write today’s grocery list on the same skin. We are palimpsests tasting of everyone we’ve ever been, and sometimes the older text bleeds through: a love note, a death certificate, a doodle of a crow unscrewing the sky.

So I write this, not to arrive anywhere, but to keep the parchment wet so yesterday’s words stay at least partially legible. If I’m lucky, tomorrow’s hunger will gnaw me open just gently enough that a future reader—maybe you—finds a faint imprint where my pulse used to be and mistakes it for flight instructions.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
